hints.junior: You know that frozen spinner feeling? Same. We're fixing how waiting *feels*, not just how fast the server is.
hints.mid: Perceived wait ≠ server TTFB. This case separates the two and shows why skeleton UI often wins on perceived speed.
hints.staff: Pair skeleton UI with RUM LCP and INP attribution — lab metrics miss the perception gap.

hints.junior: Tap Broken first — count the seconds in your head. Then tap Fixed. Same wait, different feel.
hints.mid: Toggle and notice layout stability. The spinner causes CLS when content pops in; skeleton doesn't.
hints.staff: Filmstrip: did paint wait on network or skeleton construction? That's your actual perf budget.

hints.junior: The CSS is surprisingly simple — a few divs with a shimmer animation. No special library needed.
hints.mid: React Suspense boundaries make skeleton placement declarative — fallback prop takes your skeleton.
hints.staff: Coordinate skeleton geometry with your design tokens. Mismatched sizes cause micro-CLS on swap.

anecdotes.junior: First internship we only had spinners. Skeletons felt scary until the design system shipped placeholder components.
anecdotes.mid: Spinners are easy to drop in; skeletons need agreed layout primitives across teams.
anecdotes.staff: Org-wide: define skeleton components before perf A/B tests — otherwise you're measuring implementation variance, not the pattern.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're fixing how waiting feels — not just how fast the server is. You'll be surprised how much of 'slow' is in our heads.
sections.mid: This case is about labour perception: why skeleton screens often beat spinners, even at identical load times.
sections.staff: We separate perceived performance from lab metrics, and look at what to measure in production RUM.
